editableNewDialogue.text: I’m writing a scene set at a formal gathering. How can I show a character is nervous without directly saying it?

Use physical tells and small contradictions: they smooth their sleeve repeatedly, laugh a beat late, answer too quickly, misplace an object, or over-apologize. You can also show sensory focus—counting clinks of glasses, fixating on the clock, noticing tiny details—to imply anxious energy.

Yes: give them slightly formal wording, filler phrases, or self-corrections. Examples: “Quite—sorry, yes, quite.” “I mean, of course.” “That’s not what I—what I meant.” Also, have them ask a question that doesn’t match the moment, like they’re searching for a safe topic.
How do films handle language barriers without subtitles the whole time?

Common approaches: start in the local language, then do a “language handoff” (the camera pushes in and it transitions to English); use a bilingual character as a natural interpreter; rely on context-driven exchanges (gestures, repeated key words); or keep pivotal lines in the original language and let reactions carry meaning.
Give me a quick example of the “handoff” technique.

A concierge greets guests in Italian: “Benvenuti, signori.” The traveler responds, haltingly, “Uh… prenotazione?” The concierge smiles, repeats slowly, then continues in Italian as the camera settles—mid-sentence the sound subtly shifts and the dialogue becomes English: “—and your room is on the third floor. Breakfast is included.” The audience understands they’re still speaking Italian in-world.
Random question: what’s the difference between a tea service and afternoon tea?

Afternoon tea is the meal (tea plus small bites like sandwiches, scones, pastries). A tea service is the equipment and presentation—pots, cups, tiered stand, sugar bowl, tongs, sometimes a strainer—though some venues use “tea service” to mean the set menu too.

Now I’m thinking of staging their tea party like one of those old movie stills—everything a little too perfect. I keep pulling up screenshots of Paris scenes from classic musicals just to get the vibe right, even if it’s only for describing the room.

That’s a great reference point—those films tend to have very deliberate composition, props, and lighting. If you describe the space like a carefully arranged still (symmetry, soft highlights on china, a few purposeful details), it’ll immediately read as “classic cinema” while still fitting Piper and Ingot.
I actually don’t love Audrey Hepburn’s outfits in Funny Face—those looks just aren’t my style. I mentioned Piper in one as more of a “I can picture it,” not because I liked the outfits.
Got it—so it’s not that you *like* the Funny Face outfits, you just think they’d suit Piper visually.
Exactly. It’s more neutral: they fit her vibe, but I’m not personally into them.
